retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: A Class III molar relationship occurs when the mandible is positioned anterior to the maxilla. Mandibular teeth protrude over the maxillary teeth. Class III malocclusion is distinguished by the alignment of teeth into three types. In class 3 type 1, the arch is abnormally shaped. In class 3 type 2, the mandibular teeth are tilted lingually. In class 3 type 3, the maxillary teeth are tilted lingually. [8] Ackerman and Profitt's classification system for malocclusion is used to classify and describe different types of misalignment of the teeth and jaws. It is based on Angle classification.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: In Ackerman and Profitt's classification system, malocclusion is divided into classes I, II, III, IV, V, and VI. These categories are based on the relationship between the maxillary and mandibular teeth and the jaw position. Class I: The maxillary teeth are slightly forward of the mandibular teeth, and the jaw is aligned properly. Class II: The maxillary teeth are significantly forward of the mandibular teeth, and the jaw is underdeveloped. Class III: The mandibular teeth are significantly forward of the maxillary teeth, and the jaw is overdeveloped. Class IV: The maxillary teeth are significantly behind the mandibular teeth. Class V: The maxillary teeth are significantly forward of the mandibular teeth, and the jaw is overdeveloped. Class VI: The mandibular teeth are significantly behind the maxillary teeth. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Angle class I molar classification (also known as neutroclusion) is determined by the mesiobuccal cusp of the maxillary first molar occluding with the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar. A class II molar classification (mesoclusion) is determined by the mesiobuccal cusp of the maxillary first molar, occluding mesial to the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar. Lastly, a class III molar classification is determined by the mesiobuccal cusp of the maxillary first molar occluding distal to the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar (distoclusion). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Class I malocclusion is further categorized into three types by Dewey. In Dewey type 1, the incisors are crowded, the canines are positioned labially, or both. [5] In Dewey type 2, the maxillary incisors protrude. In type 3, the anterior teeth occlude edge to edge, or there is a crossbite or both. [5] The Anderson classification further categorizes class I malocclusion into types 4 and 5. In type 4, there is a posterior crossbite, which could be unilateral or bilateral. Type 5 occlusion is a class I molar relationship with mesioversion of the permanent first molar due to the extraction of a second deciduous molar or premolar. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Acknowledged as the "father of modern orthodontics," Dr. Edward Hartley Angle established three classes of malocclusion according to the position of the mesiobuccal cusp of the upper first molar concerning the buccal groove of the lower first molar.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Several skeletal causes of malocclusion may require surgery. These include jaw discrepancies, facial asymmetry, cleft lip and palate, and craniofacial abnormalities. Additionally, although much less common, injury to the face or jaw can cause malocclusion. It is important to note that surgical treatment of malocclusion is typically only recommended in severe cases, and other treatment options, such as braces, may be tried first. A thorough evaluation by an orthodontist or oral and maxillofacial surgeon is needed to determine the most appropriate treatment approach. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: A class II division 2 is when the maxillary central incisors are palatally inclined and may be overlapped by the maxillary lateral incisors. A deep overbite and a broad maxillary arch define a class II division 2. There is a normal upper lip seal and a deep mental groove. Unlike division 1, division 2 has a normal-sized mandible. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Around 32% of individuals with malocclusion have a class II. A class II molar relationship is when the mandible is positioned retrognathic to the maxilla. Class II interarch relationship is categorized into two divisions. A Class II division 1 is when the maxillary incisors are protruded with an excessive overjet and deep overbite. The maxillary arch is often v-shaped and narrow in the canine region and broad between molar regions. Patients with a class II molar relationship division 1 have a shorter upper lip and often fail to close their anterior lip.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The following qualities define an ideal occlusion: class I molar and canine classification, 1- to 2-mm overjet, 1- to 2-mm overbite, slight to no crowding, and protective guidance movements. Furthermore, the incisal edges and buccal cusps of the teeth in the mandibular arch should align to form a smooth curve.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Malocclusion often develops when a child begins transitioning into mixed dentition. Malocclusion can cause speech problems, difficulty eating, changes in facial structure, and tongue and cheek biting. Since there is no known etiology behind malocclusion, an interprofessional team approach is crucial to allow for an earlier diagnosis. Diagnosis is standardized with the use of classification systems and radiographs. Orthodontists use several methods to treat malocclusion, including palatal expanders, spacers, braces, and clear aligners. This activity defines and distinguishes the different types of malocclusion seen in orthodontics and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in diagnosing and utilizing various techniques to treat malocclusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Orthodontics seeks to achieve esthetic and functional improvement via mechanical therapy that moves teeth into a more ideal position. Determining the ideal dental position for each patient depends on several factors, such as the facial profile, facial balance, and aesthetic concerns. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of malocclusion is often multifactorial, mainly influenced by genetic and environmental factors. Inherited factors are also believed to be involved, but their exact role is yet to be fully understood.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: While various methods have been proven to treat malocclusion successfully, newer technologies are still being tested to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of treatment. Perhaps in the near future, the utilization of low-level laser therapy on orthodontic movement, for example, will be a common practice in orthodontic offices. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Environmental factors can play a role in the development of malocclusion as well. This is often seen in children who habitually suck on their thumbs. When pressure is placed against the roof of the mouth, the lower incisors and maxillary molars are pushed lingually, and the upper incisors are pushed labially. Furthermore, the jaw is positioned downward, which allows for posterior teeth to erupt, resulting in a separation of the incisors. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of malocclusion. Describe the etiology behind malocclusion. Identify the different classes of malocclusion. Apply best practices when treating malocclusion. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: While most malocclusion cases have an unknown etiology, the correlation between genetics and malocclusion has been studied extensively. Data suggests that a specific malocclusion, Class III mandibular prognathism, is passed down through generations via specific growth factors and genetic markers. Dating back to the 1300s, the heritability pattern of mandibular prognathism can be seen in the European royal Hapsburg family. Nicknamed the “Hapsburg Jaw,” interbreeding between different family members allowed the genes encoding mandibular prognathism to be expressed over multiple generations. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1930
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system” functions as a classification label for the broader category “other or therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system.” Within this classified group, the specific intervention “Reopening of osteotomy site of facial bone” is regarded as a related and possibly synonymous procedure, indicating that it belongs to the same set of musculoskeletal therapeutic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: diverse injury types (intracranial, skeletal, facial), their specific anatomical subcategories, corresponding treatment modalities, and long‑term sequelae are interrelated, while also incorporating psychosocial determinants such as aggression that may influence injury occurrence and outcome. This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2783
retrieved_docs.content: Physical therapy exercises; manipulation; and other procedures are identified as a distinct category that classifies itself, indicating that these therapeutic interventions are grouped together under a single classification for clinical and therapeutic purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2204
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system is a classification term that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system, grouping together various interventions aimed at treating musculoskeletal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2056
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical Manipulation is a broad category of operative interventions that includes specific techniques such as Application of Therapeutic Agent and Manual Conversion of Position. Application of Therapeutic Agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication or other therapeutic substances, while Manual Conversion of Position involves the physical repositioning of tissues, organs, or skeletal structures during a procedure. Both Application of Therapeutic Agent and Manual Conversion of Position are considered child concepts within Surgical Manipulation, illustrating the diverse range of actions that fall under this overarching surgical domain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injuries or internal injuries are associated with open wounds (جرح مفتوح), indicating that such traumatic forces often result in visible tissue disruption.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1853
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on joints is a classification that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on joints.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: legal interventions, cervical spine fractures, cutting/piercing mechanisms, and neurotoxic tissue damage through shared classifications, hierarchical inverse‑isa and cause‑of relationships, and common morphological outcomes such as تَلَف الأنسجة, providing clinicians with an integrated framework for coding, diagnosing, and managing a wide spectrum of injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1736
retrieved_docs.content: Traction, splints, and other wound‑care modalities are understood to have a broader relationship with the term “الجر,” the Arabic word for traction, indicating that these interventions are encompassed within the larger concept of traction in wound management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1781
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injury or internal injury is associated with the medical condition referred to as إصابة (injury), indicating that such traumatic forces commonly result in injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 139
retrieved_docs.content: crash, is associated with extensive tissue damage, reinforcing the link between high‑energy fractures, tissue injury, and neurotoxic effects. Overall, fracture (كسر) integrates late sequelae, specific anatomical fracture types, associated tissue damage, co‑occurring injuries, procedural interventions, and psychosocial classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2833
retrieved_docs.content: The category “الإجراءات العلاجية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (Therapeutic procedures, not classified elsewhere) includes “جراحة” (Surgery) as a child concept, indicating that surgery is considered a specific type of therapeutic procedure within this broader, uncategorized therapeutic classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2430
retrieved_docs.content: Other miscellaneous procedures are classified as a subset of other nonoperative procedures, indicating that they fall under the broader category of nonoperative interventions.
